prompt:
A highly detailed, top-down view of a massive ancient tree cross-section filling the entire frame, as if photographed lying flat on a dark museum surface. The cross-section shows clearly visible concentric growth rings radiating from the center outward to rough, weathered bark at the edges.
Embedded within the wood grain are delicate natural history illustrations in the style of 19th-century scientific zoological plates — Audubon, Ernst Haeckel, Maria Sibylla Merian. The illustrations are rendered as if painted directly into the wood surface, following the curvature of the rings.
In the innermost rings (center 25%), wildlife illustrations are extraordinarily vivid and densely packed: a tiger, an elephant, a hummingbird, a sea turtle, a frog, butterflies, a whale, an eagle, tropical fish, and many smaller creatures. Colors are rich — deep oranges, emerald greens, sapphire blues — as if illuminated from within the warm honey-gold wood grain. This zone teems with life.
In the middle rings (25% to 50% from center), animal illustrations begin to fade. Colors shift from vivid to sepia and ochre. Some animals appear half-drawn, dissolving into the wood grain. Density of creatures noticeably decreases. A visible zone of transition and loss.
In the outer rings (outermost 50%), almost all animals have vanished. Only faint pencil-sketch outlines and ghostly traces remain — spectral impressions of creatures that once existed. The very outermost rings are completely empty, showing nothing but bare, stressed wood grain with tight rings and hairline cracks.
The bark edge is rough, raw, cracked — freshly felled. Overall mood is elegiac and haunting. Lighting is soft, even, museum-quality. Color palette transitions from warm rich gold at center to cool pale ash at edges.
No text. No labels. No watermarks. No charts. No thermometers, globes, or maps. No polar bears. No split-earth imagery. No fantasy elements. No glowing effects. Naturalistic scientific illustration style. Aspect ratio 1:1. Ultra-high detail.
insight: Monitored wildlife populations have declined by 73% on average between 1970 and 2020
